front: What is the interrupt vector address for External Interrupt 0 (INT0) in 8051?
back: 0003H is the vector address for INT0. This is where the CPU jumps when INT0 is triggered.

front: What is the purpose of the IE register in 8051?
back: The IE (Interrupt Enable) register enables or disables individual interrupts. Bit 7 (EA) enables/disables all interrupts when set to 1 or 0.

front: What does the RETI instruction do in 8051?
back: RETI (Return from Interrupt) restores the program counter from the stack and restores the interrupt logic to allow nested interrupts. It must be the last instruction in an ISR.

front: What is interrupt latency in microcontrollers?
back: Interrupt latency is the time between the interrupt request (IRQ) signal and the start of the ISR execution. It depends on the current machine cycle and interrupt enable status.

front: How many external interrupts does the 8051 have?
back: The 8051 has two external interrupts: INT0 (P3.2) and INT1 (P3.3), mapped to vector addresses 0003H and 0013H respectively.

front: What is the IP (Interrupt Priority) register used for?
back: The IP register sets priority levels for interrupts. Higher priority interrupts can interrupt lower priority ISR. Bits: PS (serial), PT1 (timer1), PX1 (external1), PT0 (timer0), PX0 (external0).

front: What happens to the stack pointer when an interrupt is accepted in 8051?
back: The 8051 automatically pushes the current PC (Program Counter) onto the stack. The user must manually save other registers (ACC, PSW, B, DPTR) if needed in the ISR.

front: What is the difference between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts?
back: Level-triggered: Interrupt is active as long as the pin is low. Edge-triggered: Interrupt triggers on high-to-low transition. Set via TCON register bits (IT0, IT1).

front: What is the vector address for Timer 0 overflow interrupt?
back: 000BH is the vector address for Timer 0 overflow interrupt in the 8051.

front: What is the function of the EA bit in IE register?
back: EA (Enable All) bit when set to 0 disables all interrupts globally. When set to 1, individual interrupts can be enabled via their respective bits in the IE register.
Yes, 8051 supports nested interrupts, but only if the higher priority interrupt is triggered while a lower priority ISR is executing. The ISR must enable interrupts (EA=1) for nesting.

front: What registers must be saved in an ISR to preserve program state?
back: Critical registers to save: ACC (Accumulator), PSW (Program Status Word), B register, DPTR, and any other register modified by the ISR that the main program depends on.

front: What is the vector address for Serial Port interrupt?
back: 0023H is the vector address for Serial Port interrupt in the 8051.

front: What is polling vs interrupt-driven approach?
back: Polling: CPU continuously checks for events (wasteful). Interrupt-driven: CPU executes main program; hardware notifies when event occurs (efficient for time-critical operations).

front: How many machine cycles does an interrupt response take in 8051?
back: Interrupt response takes 2-4 machine cycles depending on the current instruction. The fastest response is 2 cycles if the current instruction is at the end of its execution.
